The surgeon searches for the tattoo marking the distal extent of the right-sided colon polyposis but does not find it on the exposed transverse colon.

The surgeon positions the patient with the right side elevated to facilitate a medial to lateral approach, moving the small bowel aside. A small tattoo is visible in the mesentery, marking the area of interest.
The surgeon attempts to tent the ileocolic pedicle but struggles to expose the anatomical structures, indicating difficulty in visualizing the surgical field.

The surgeon begins lateral mobilization of the colon, aiming to elevate the ileocolic pedicle for better exposure. The colon is manipulated from lateral to medial, deviating from the usual medial to lateral approach.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the right colon, ensuring the ureter is identified and checked before proceeding with the dissection.

The surgeon uses an inferior approach to mobilize the cecum, with the camera positioned in the suprapubic port. The left lower quadrant and left parambulical port are utilized for instrument manipulation.
The surgeon continues dissecting around the ileocolic pedicle, visible to the right of the ligature, while attempting to locate the duodenum and navigate around the flexure.

The surgeon continues to mobilize the right colon, detaching it from the abdominal wall and the liver bed.

Whenever I hear the comment about how challenging the case is going to be, that it should be done open, and then I see what we're ending up doing here, I always say to myself, how would this have been easier open? I suppose if you made a laparotomy from the xiphoid to the pubis and you had a giant retractor in there and numerous hands holding things out of the way, that would be easier. Um, but I don't know if that's really in the best interest of the patient. So I think that if you can struggle through cases like this minimally invasive and give them the benefit, I think that these are the patients who benefit the most.
The surgeon lifts the ileocolic pedicle, using sponges to hold surrounding tissues, and creates a window around the pedicle following the completed lateral dissection.

The surgeon identifies the ileocolic pedicle and proceeds with dissection, safely ligating the pedicle despite not achieving the highest ligation. Thirty-two lymph nodes are removed during this dissection, all of which are negative. The surgeon then targets the right branch of the middle colic artery, noting the absence of a right colic artery as confirmed by pre-operative imaging.
The surgeon approaches the colon margin marked by the tattoo, reaching the bell wall as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon splits the omentum at the level of the distal margin, removing the omentum en bloc with the specimen.
The surgeon proceeds to take the remaining small bowel mesentery, followed by the use of indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence imaging to visualize and staple the margins.

The surgeon inserts the stapler through the suprapubic port to take the small bowel, placing the specimen temporarily above the liver. The surgeon then lines up to perform an isoparastaltic intracorporeal anastomosis and begins making a colotomy.
The surgeon introduces the stapler with a vascular load to secure the common channel, preparing for the anastomosis.

The surgeon observes a clear open lumen with no bleeding and proceeds to sew the common channel shut. Using two needle drivers, the surgeon alternates between right and left hands to suture, adapting to the angles for optimal technique.
And that's what I did in this case.
The surgeon switches to right-handed suturing to perform an over-and-over technique along the cumular neurotomy.

The surgeon reinforces the sutured area at the top of the colotomy, using left-handed suturing due to the angle being more appropriate. The surgeon experiences difficulty in grabbing the needle during the process.
The surgeon switches back to right-handed suturing, using a 3-0, 9-inch V-Lock suture to run it up and then back along the surgical site.

The surgeon begins suturing at the near corner to prevent inward rolling of the tissue, securing the anastomosis with clips and covering it with omentum. The specimen is retrieved through a fan and steel incision at the port site, with visible adhesions left untouched.
